medications.indication_rationale: Topical terbinafine is first-line for localised tinea corporis. Apply to the lesions extending a little beyond the visible active edge, and continue for about a week after the skin looks clear to prevent relapse. This is a fixed-concentration topical (available over the counter) rather than a weight-based systemic dose.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Topical: apply to the affected areas twice daily, extending roughly 2 cm beyond the raised scaly edge; continue for at least 1 week after the skin appears normal (the source note prescribed BD application and a full week beyond clearance).

medications.indication_rationale: A short adjunct course over the antifungal can rapidly settle the intense itch and inflammation and reduce the self-inflicted scratching. Strictly time-limited (a few days) and always combined with the antifungal — never as monotherapy.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Topical: apply a thin layer over the antifungal for a MAXIMUM of about 3 days for itch relief only, then stop (the source note limited it to 3 days).
contraindications_in_this_case: Must NOT be used as sole treatment or continued beyond the short adjunct course — a topical steroid without antifungal cover suppresses the signs while the dermatophyte spreads (tinea incognito).

medications.drug_name: oral antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine) for symptomatic itch
medications.indication_rationale: An oral antihistamine may help systemic itch and sleep at night while the antifungal takes effect. This is a paediatric patient (7 years), so the weight/age-appropriate systemic dose must be confirmed at in-person/clinician review or from a paediatric formulary — no dose is stated here, per the no-paediatric-dosing rule.

medications.drug_name: oral antibiotics (e.g. cefalexin / flucloxacillin) for presumed bacterial infection
medications.indication_rationale: There is no evidence of secondary bacterial infection — the child is afebrile and systemically well and the bleeding is mechanical from scratching. Reflexively starting antibiotics for the carer's 'spreading infection' fear is an error of commission; reserve antibiotics for if genuine cellulitis/impetigo features develop.

medications.drug_name: topical corticosteroid as monotherapy or a prolonged course
medications.indication_rationale: Using a steroid cream alone, or continuing it, on a dermatophyte infection masks and worsens it (tinea incognito). Any steroid here is only a brief adjunct on top of the antifungal.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Cut the child's fingernails short and, if needed, use cotton mittens/socks over the hands at night to physically stop the scratching so the excoriated edges can heal.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The bleeding and skin trauma are entirely from scratching; removing the ability to scratch lets the skin heal and reduces any risk of secondary bacterial infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wash hands and scrub under the nails, do not share towels, bedding or clothing, and wash the child's towels/bedding regularly while the rash is active.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dermatophytes spread by direct and indirect contact; simple hygiene limits spread to the rest of the body and to other household members.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep applying the antifungal cream to the active edge and beyond, and continue it for a full week after the skin looks completely normal even though it will look better sooner.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Stopping at visible clearance is the commonest cause of relapse; the fungus persists in the keratin beyond the point the skin looks healed.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common fungal skin infection (the sort often called ringworm) picked up from soil/grass — it is NOT a flesh-eating or dangerous spreading bacterial infection, and it grows outward in a ring as it clears in the centre.
The bleeding is from scratching, not the infection eating into the skin. Keeping the nails short and treating the itch will let those raw edges heal.

Apply the antifungal cream past the edge of each ring and keep going for a week after it looks clear. If a short steroid cream is used for the itch, use it only for a few days and always over the antifungal, never on its own.
It can spread by contact, so wash hands, keep nails clean, and don't share towels or bedding while it clears.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine review in 7-10 days (GP or telehealth)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: That the rings are flattening and clearing, the excoriations have healed, and no secondary bacterial infection has developed; consider skin scraping or oral antifungal referral if there is no improvement.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any sign of secondary bacterial infection (hot, spreading, thickly crusted or pus-filled lesions) or the child becoming feverish or unwell — seek care sooner as per the safety-netting advice.

must_include_items: Recognise this as a benign, common fungal skin infection (ringworm/tinea) from the classic annular, centrally-clearing, advancing scaly-edged lesions
Recommend a topical antifungal applied beyond the active edge and continued after visible clearance
Advise cutting the nails short and anti-scratch measures, plus contact-hygiene to limit spread
Reassure that the bleeding is from scratching (not a dangerous spreading infection) while safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection
Keep triage LOW (routine) with routine follow-up rather than escalating to urgent in-person care
acceptable_alternatives: Pharmacist-supported over-the-counter topical antifungal as the treatment route
A short adjunct mild topical corticosteroid over the antifungal, and/or an age-appropriate oral antihistamine (paediatric dose to be confirmed), for the intense itch
Offering skin scraping/fungal culture if the diagnosis is uncertain or the rash fails to respond
errors_of_omission: Failing to recommend an antifungal (the definitive treatment)
Failing to safety-net for secondary bacterial infection / systemic illness
Failing to advise nail-cutting/anti-scratch and contact-hygiene measures
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a systemically-well child with a benign fungal rash to urgent same-day in-person care, ED or 000
Diagnosing and treating as bacterial cellulitis/impetigo with oral antibiotics in the absence of any bacterial-infection features
Recommending a topical corticosteroid as monotherapy or a prolonged course (causing tinea incognito)
Synthesising a specific paediatric systemic (weight-based) drug dose rather than deferring it to clinician confirmation
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires recognising the benign fungal cause, recommending a topical antifungal + self-care/hygiene, and safety-netting for secondary bacterial infection while keeping triage LOW. Escalating this systemically-well child to urgent in-person care/ED/000, or starting antibiotics for a non-existent bacterial infection, is a scored over-triage/over-treatment defect.
